text: Although preaching in a prophetic style is a well-known tradition in South Africa—think of Desmond Tutu in the context of the anti-apartheid movement—after 1994 the country has had to face an immense new injustice: the social exclusion and extreme poverty of more than half of the population. With descriptions of the huge inequality in the country, the corruption in the handling of social grants, the falling apart of our education system and many more indicators, the researcher portrays through qualitative interviews with youth living in our township areas the feelings of disillusionment and deprivation of the poor and the unemployed. This crisis can and should be addressed through prophetic preaching as a homiletic genre that is defined by a fundamental alienation in which the prophet acts as the mouth of the voiceless. The conditions for effective prophetic preaching are identified as: a) Social solidarity with the poor and the oppressed, based on sound and on-going social analysis; b) An adequate hermeneutic that is able to lift Scripture from being a relic of the past into becoming a word-event which functions as an event which address human existential conditions; and c) A concrete form of support and responsibility from the local churches in the townships and from the ecumenical world of churches in general. Prophetic preaching therefore must become a structural part of the function of the church in the age in which we live, so as to meet the challenge of inequality and to carry on the struggle for a better human existence.
explanation: The passage shows a highly structured, abstract academic voice with general claims and no personal anecdotes, a pattern common in AI-generated drafts. Occasional awkward phrasing and slightly uniform rhetoric further tilt the likelihood toward AI involvement, though a careful human-authored piece could resemble it.
